query: diagnosis of cryptosporidium in HIV patients

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptosporidiosis: ART must be initiated in patients with a goal to restore CD4+ cells count greater than 100 cells/mm^3 as this leads to the resolution of clinical cryptosporidiosis. Diarrheal losses should be adequately repleted either orally or by the IV route. Multiple agents including nitazoxanide, spiramycin, paromomycin, bovine dialyzable leukocyte extract, and bovine hyperimmune colostrum are studied, but none of these are effective consistently without concurrent ART use. [32] Microsporidiosis: ART must be initiated in patients with a goal to restore CD4+ cells count over 100 cells/mm^3 as this leads to the resolution of clinical microsporidiosis. JC virus infection: There is no definitive treatment available for JCV. Initiation of ART to reverse immunosuppression is the main therapy in these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Opportunistic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Toxoplasma gondii :  Patients with CD4+ counts less than 100 cells/mm 3 who have positive test results for toxoplasma antibodies require chemoprophylaxis . [26]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Microsporidiosis:  These microsporidia can be detected by light microscopy using stains such as Chromotrope 2R, calcofluor white and Uvitex 2B which can help differentiate between the spores of the microsporidia and the cells and debris (e.g., stool). In patients with gastrointestinal symptoms, if the stool testing is negative and the infection is suspected, small bowel biopsy must be considered. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS (Nursing) -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Eventually, untreated HIV progresses to advanced disease, which manifests itself with a myriad of opportunistic infections and conditions. Acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) can be diagnosed when specific AIDS-defining conditions are noted, regardless of the CD4+ lymphocyte cell count. These AIDS-defining conditions, as outlined by the CDC, include: [12] Candidiasis of the digestive tract (other than thrush) Candidiasis of the pulmonary tract Invasive cervical cancer Extrapulmonary or disseminated coccidioidomycosis, histoplasmosis, or cryptococcosis Chronic intestinal cryptosporidiosis or isosporiasis Cytomegalovirus retinitis Kaposi sarcoma HIV encephalitis Primary lymphoma of the brain Burkitt lymphoma Mycobacterial infections Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptococcus neoformans: In patients with meningitis or meningoencephalitis, Cryptococcal antigen is easily detected in the CSF, as well as in the serum. Positive blood cultures present in up to 75% of HIV positive patients with cryptococcal meningitis. [21] Cryptosporidiosis: Microscopic identification of the oocytes in stool or tissue as well as their detection by direct immunofluorescence or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Various parasitic infections can lead to acute gastroenteritis, including Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora, Isospora, and Mycobacterium . In cases where parasitic causes are suspected, stool samples should be tested for ova and parasites, and appropriate antimicrobial treatment should be initiated if tests are positive. [14] In patients with acute diarrhea, abdominal pathology should always be ruled out. Acute appendicitis, diverticulitis, inflammatory bowel disease, bowel obstruction, irritable bowel syndrome, ischemic bowel disease, laxative abuse, diabetes, malabsorption syndromes, scleroderma, and celiac sprue can all present with symptoms similar to that of rotavirus infection. When the diagnostic evaluation has failed to yield a cause for acute diarrhea, noninfectious extraintestinal causes should be considered. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- History and Physical -- AIDS
retrieved_docs.content: Eventually, HIV progresses to advanced disease, particularly if left untreated or inadequately treated. AIDS is diagnosed when specific AIDS-defining conditions are noted, regardless of the CD4+ count. These AIDS-defining conditions, outlined by the CDC, include: Candidiasis of the digestive tract (other than thrush) Candidiasis of the pulmonary tract Invasive cervical cancer Extrapulmonary or disseminated coccidioidomycosis, histoplasmosis, or cryptococcosis, including cryptococcal meningitis Chronic intestinal cryptosporidiosis or isosporiasis Cytomegalovirus retinitis Kaposi sarcoma HIV encephalitis and HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder Tuberculosis Primary lymphoma of the brain Non-Hodgkin lymphoma Burkitt lymphoma Mycobacterial infections Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy Salmonella septicemia HIV-associated wasting syndrome [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptosporidiosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of cryptosporidiosis is with an interprofessional team that consists of the emergency department physician, infectious disease specialist, internist, nurse practitioner and a pharmacist. Once the disorder is diagnosed, besides hydration, in immunocompetent patients, nitazoxanide is the treatment of choice and FDA-approved for the treatment of cryptosporidiosis. Unfortunately, it is not always successful in reducing symptoms. Paromomycin or azithromycin may also be used. However, in immunocompromised patients, these treatments may fail without immune reconstitution. Intact cellular immunity is essential for resolution of symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-2 Infection -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: HIV-2 should be considered in those with epidemiologic risk factors or an opportunistic infection. As such, the differential is broad and includes a range of diseases caused by opportunistic and sexually transmitted pathogens, including: HIV-1 infection Dual infection with HIV-1 and HIV-2 Hepatitis B Hepatitis C Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia Toxoplasma gondii encephalitis Cryptosporidiosis Mycobacterium tuberculosis Nontuberculous mycobacterial infection Bartonellosis Syphilis Mucocutaneous candidiasis Cryptococcosis Histoplasmosis Coccidioidomycosis Aspergillosis Cytomegalovirus Herpes simplex virus Varicella zoster virus Human herpesvirus-8/Kaposi sarcoma Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptosporidiosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptosporidium is the second major cause of moderate to severe diarrhea in children younger than two and an important cause of mortality worldwide. It occurs most commonly during waterborne epidemics and in immunocompromised hosts. Cryptosporidiosis may be underrecognized and underdiagnosed. Additionally, treatments are suboptimum, and we do not currently have robust preventative measures.This activity reviews the cause, pathophysiology and presentation of cryptosporidiosis and stresses the importance of a interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Some more emphasized common AIDS-defining illnesses would include: candida esophagitis, invasive cervical cancer, PCP pneumonia, disseminated TB, Kaposi sarcoma, and Cryptosporidium diarrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptosporidiosis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Contaminated water sources or fecal-oral transfer frequently spread Cryptosporidium . A recent Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) report shows that the incidence of Cryptosporidium outbreaks has increased significantly in the United States. Many outbreaks in the United States have been associated with the contamination of recreational and municipal water supplies. Outbreaks in daycare centers have also been reported, likely due to fecal-oral transmission. Transmission during anal intercourse has also been reported due to fecal-oral transmission. Cryptosporidium is also associated with seasonal changes and malnutrition. Studies have shown that higher temperature and more rainfall are associated with an increased risk of cryptosporidiosis. The number of reported cases has increased in recent years with increasing clinical recognition and testing. It is more common in low resource settings, with 5% to 10% of immunocompetent hosts shedding the organism in low resource countries as opposed to approximately 1% in high resource settings. Animal exposure, particularly in cats and cattle, is also associated with increased risk of infection. Children and the elderly are most commonly diagnosed with cryptosporidiosis. Patients with acquired (human immunodeficiency virus, chemotherapy, immunosuppressant medications,) or congenital (hypogammaglobulinemia; IgA deficiency) immunodeficiency are at highest risk of infection and for prolonged or severe illness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 952
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis, a combined inflammatory condition of the stomach and duodenum, are clinically associated with the presence of تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى (bloody diarrhea) and with a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (positive HIV test). In addition, gastritis and duodenitis are considered a parent condition to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (inflammatory bowel disease), indicating that patients with these upper‑gastrointestinal inflammations may also exhibit lower‑gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloody stools, may have underlying HIV infection, and may be at risk for or progress to inflammatory bowel disease. Recognizing these interconnections can guide clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2409
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis, a combined inflammatory condition of the stomach and duodenum, is clinically associated with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), indicating that individuals infected with HIV are at increased risk for developing these gastrointestinal lesions. In addition, ulcerative gastritis (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ) is considered a specific subtype or “child” condition of gastritis and duodenitis, representing a more severe form of mucosal damage. Recognizing the link between HIV infection and gastritis/duodenitis, as well as the relationship of ulcerative gastritis to the broader disease spectrum, is important for comprehensive patient assessment, targeted screening, and appropriate management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض, and a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي is also clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض. Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical link between this symptom complex and HIV infection, indicating that patients presenting with low white‑blood‑cell counts should be evaluated for underlying HIV disease and related symptomatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1170
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is classified as a subtype of فقر الدم (anemia). فقر الدم is clinically associated with both gastritis and duodenitis and with الشعور بالضعف (feeling of weakness). Gastritis and duodenitis, in turn, are linked to a positive فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (HIV test) result and give rise to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (peptic ulcer disease) as a downstream condition. The symptom complex malaise and fatigue maps to الشعور بالضعف, النوام, and is additionally related to الضعف العصبي (neurological weakness). Together, these relationships highlight that patients with anemia—particularly acute posthemorrhagic anemia—may present with gastrointestinal inflammation, weakness, and nonspecific fatigue, and that such gastrointestinal pathology can be associated with HIV infection and may progress to peptic ulcer disease. Recognizing the overlap among anemia, gastritis/duodenitis, HIV status, and the mapped symptoms can improve diagnostic accuracy and guide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with gastritis and duodenitis, indicating that the presence of this symptom complex may suggest underlying inflammation of the stomach and duodenum. Gastritis and duodenitis themselves are clinically associated with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), highlighting a potential link between these gastrointestinal conditions and HIV infection that clinicians should consider when evaluating patients. In addition, gastritis and duodenitis have a child condition identified as الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, suggesting that the inflammatory process may extend to or give rise to this specific gastric pathology. Recognizing these interconnected relationships can aid healthcare providers in comprehensive assessment, appropriate testing for HIV, and targeted management of related gastrointestinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis is recognized as a specific form of gastritis, with gastritis listed as its parent condition. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis is associated with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), indicating that patients presenting with this gastrointestinal inflammation may have a higher likelihood of HIV infection. Additionally, gastritis and duodenitis can give rise to a more severe ulcerative disorder, identified as التهاب المعدة التآكلي, reflecting a potential progression from inflammatory disease to ulcer formation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians consider HIV screening in patients with gastritis‑duodenitis and monitor for possible development of ulcerative complications.

The presence of تغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى is further linked to anemia from blood loss (فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم) within the SMQ bleeding group. Malaise and fatigue are grouped under signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, mapped to sensations of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), lethargy (النوام), and neurological weakness (الضعف العصبي). The categories diseases and injuries and the broader Symptoms concept are organized under the parent signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting their interrelated nature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2805
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة); in patients with AIDS, pulmonary opportunistic infections and other HIV‑related lung complications frequently produce atypical or abnormal radiographic appearances, making chest imaging an important diagnostic and monitoring tool in the management of this immunocompromised condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1229
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis are gastrointestinal inflammatory conditions that are clinically associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى (bloody diarrhea) and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (a positive HIV test). The presence of gastritis and duodenitis may also be a precursor to or coexist with الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (inflammatory bowel disease). Additionally, تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى is clinically associated with فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم (anemia resulting from blood loss), reflecting the potential for significant hemorrhage to cause blood‑loss anemia. Recognizing these interrelationships is important for comprehensive evaluation and management of patients presenting with upper gastrointestinal inflammation, especially when accompanied by hemorrhagic diarrhea, HIV infection, or signs of anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1166
retrieved_docs.content: Stomach cancer, referred to as “cancer of stomach” and also named سرطان المعدة in Arabic, is clinically associated with a low white‑blood‑cell count (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). This leukopenia is further linked to a broader SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and is commonly observed in patients who have a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي). Consequently, leukopenia serves as a shared clinical finding that connects gastrointestinal malignancy, HIV infection, and a constellation of signs and symptoms, underscoring its importance for diagnosis, monitoring, and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1188
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis are clinically associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى (bloody diarrhea) and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (a positive HIV test), indicating that patients presenting with these upper gastrointestinal inflammations may also exhibit hemorrhagic diarrhea and may have underlying HIV infection. In the disease hierarchy, gastritis and duodenitis has a child entity, الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, reflecting a close relationship with inflammatory bowel disease of the colon. The presence of تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى is further linked to فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم (anemia due to blood loss), underscoring that chronic or severe bloody diarrhea can lead to iron‑deficiency anemia secondary to ongoing hemorrhage. فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم is classified as a member of مصطلحات النزف (باستثناء المصطلحات المختبرية) (SMQ), the standardized terminology group for bleeding‑related adverse events, highlighting its relevance within broader bleeding disorder reporting and patient safety monitoring. Together, these relationships illustrate how upper gastrointestinal inflammation, infectious status, lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and resultant hematologic consequences are interconnected in clinical assessment and management.

Gastritis and duodenitis, described as inflammatory forms of the stomach and duodenum (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة, اضطراب معدي), can progress to peptic ulcer disease (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ). Clinical observations associate gastritis and duodenitis with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) and with hemorrhagic ulceration (تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى). Hemorrhagic infiltration (تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى) is further linked to anemia due to blood loss (فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم). These interrelated drug classifications, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and disease associations underscore the importance of careful medication selection, awareness of thrombotic risk, and comprehensive assessment of gastrointestinal inflammation, HIV status, and bleeding complications in patient care.

Both قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض and gastritis and duodenitis are also clinically associated with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (a positive HIV test). In addition, gastritis and duodenitis has a child entity, الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (peptic ulcer disease), indicating that peptic ulcer disease is a subcategory of the upper‑gastrointestinal inflammatory process. Together, these relationships suggest that in the context of HIV infection, a symptom complex may include leukopenia, gastritis, duodenitis, and related peptic ulcer disease, highlighting the interconnected nature of hematologic and gastrointestinal manifestations in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat اضطراب معيدي (intestinal disorder) and الإسهال (diarrhea). Gastritis and duodenitis are clinically associated with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (a positive HIV test) and are classified as a child condition of التهاب المعدة التآكلي (peptic ulcer disease) while also being a child of اضطراب معيدي (intestinal disorder). This network of relationships highlights that bismuth‑based medications can address common gastrointestinal symptoms such as diarrhea and broader intestinal disturbances, and that inflammatory conditions of the stomach and duodenum are linked to HIV infection, fall under the umbrella of intestinal disorders, and can progress to peptic ulcer disease, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnosis and treatment in gastrointestinal and infectious disease care.
